mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz Vito petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Vito petrol engine typically costs £800–£6,500 supply only depending on which generation (M111, M112, M271 or M274). Used units are available from £800–£2,500. Fitting adds £1,200–£2,500 depending on the workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your Vito.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz Vito petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which generation. The M112 V6 is widely regarded as 'a very durable and reliable engine'. The M111 is also robust but ageing. The M271 is the least reliable — timing chain issues are well-documented. The M274 is modern and efficient, but thermostat housing and PCV issues have been reported.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz Vito petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: M111: head gasket failure and MAF sensor issues. M112: crank pulley disintegration and PCV system leaks. M271: timing chain stretch and camshaft adjuster wear. M274: thermostat housing failure and PCV valve issues. The M112 is the most reliable of the four.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz Vito petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Vito values range from £2,000–£15,000 depending on condition and generation. A £800–£6,500 reconditioned engine is 15–50% of the vehicle's value — apply the Mercedes 70% scrap threshold. If the van is a cherished example with good bodywork, replacement is often worthwhile.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz Vito petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. W638 Vito (1996–2003) uses M111; W639 Vito (2003–2010) uses M112; W639 Vito (2010–2014) uses M271; W447 Vito (2014–present) uses M274. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M271 timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The timing chain and camshaft adjusters can be replaced — they are wear items. Replacement costs £1,200–£2,500. If the chain fails and jumps timing, the engine is typically destroyed. A preventative chain and adjuster replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£2,000+). At the first sign of rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz Vito Petrol Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current Vito petrol market. The M111 is the most affordable to replace; the M274 commands the highest premium. Reconditioned engines from specialist suppliers typically start from around £1,500 supply only. See the Era Map above for generation-specific context.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Vito petrol engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The longitudinal layout makes access relatively straightforward, though the V6 and turbocharged engines add complexity.

cta.label: → Compare Vito petrol engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: M111 Head Gasket and MAF Sensor Failure (W638 — 1996–2003)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, overheating, white smoke from exhaust, rough idle, loss of power, engine warning light
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 (head gasket replacement or MAF sensor replacement)
problems.recommendation: The M111 is a robust engine, but head gasket failure can occur at higher mileages. MAF sensor failure is also a common issue, causing rough running and loss of power. If you experience coolant loss or rough running, have the engine diagnosed promptly. A head gasket replacement is well-understood by Mercedes specialists.

failureLink.label: Read the full head gasket failure guide

problems.title: M112 Crank Pulley Failure and PCV System Leaks (W639 — 2003–2010)
problems.symptoms: Vibrations, loss of power, oil leaks, burning oil smell, visible oil residue on the engine
problems.replacementCost: £1,800–£3,500 (if crank pulley failure causes engine damage)
problems.recommendation: The M112 V6 is "a very durable and reliable engine", but the crank pulley rubber can disintegrate over time. PCV system leaks are also common — the valve cover breathers "always leak after a while". If you notice vibrations or oil leaks, have the crank pulley and PCV system inspected. These are well-understood repairs for any Mercedes specialist.

failureLink.label: Read the full crank pulley failure guide

problems.title: M271 Timing Chain and Camshaft Adjuster Wear (W639 — 2010–2014)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking from the front of the engine, engine warning light, cam timing errors
problems.repairCost: £1,200–£2,500 (timing chain, tensioner and camshaft adjuster replacement)
problems.replacementCost: £2,000–£4,000 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: this is the single biggest risk on the M271

problems.recommendation: The M271's timing chain and camshaft adjusters are well-documented weak points. Rattling on cold start is the classic symptom. If you hear any rattling, book an inspection immediately. A preventative chain and adjuster replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: M274 Thermostat Housing and PCV Valve Issues (W447 — 2014–present)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, rising temperature gauge, engine warning light, rough idle, whistling noise from the engine
problems.recommendation: The M274 is a modern and efficient engine, but the thermostat housing can fail, causing coolant loss and overheating. PCV valve failure can also occur, leading to rough idle and whistling noises. If you notice coolant loss or rough running, have the thermostat housing and PCV system inspected.

failureLink.label: Read the full thermostat failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Vito Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Reseal or replace breathers — prevent oil loss
Labour-intensive but far cheaper than a full engine replacement
If chain jumps and valves hit pistons, replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Vito Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * Post-2014 M274 with full service history — the most modern and efficient petrol Vito
* W639 M112 V6 (2003–2010) — widely regarded as one of Mercedes' most reliable V6 engines
* W638 M111 with documented head gasket and MAF work — the common weaknesses addressed
* Full service history with regular oil changes — all Vito petrol engines reward good maintenance
avoidIf: * M271 with any rattling on cold start — timing chain wear is likely
* M112 with severe vibrations — crank pulley disintegration is imminent
* M111 with coolant loss and no diagnosis — head gasket failure is likely
* Poor service history — all Vito petrol engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Vito petrol range spans three decades and four engine families. The M111 (1996–2003) is robust but ageing — head gasket and MAF issues are common. The M112 V6 (2003–2010) is widely regarded as one of Mercedes' most reliable engines — smooth, durable, but with crank pulley and PCV system weaknesses. The M271 (2010–2014) is the least reliable of the four — timing chain issues are well-documented. The M274 (2014–present) is modern and efficient, but thermostat housing and PCV issues have been reported. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and the Vito petrol will reward you with reliable, versatile performance.

cta.label: → Compare Vito petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz Vito petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start (M271) — Listen for rattling from the front of the engine (timing chain warning)
2. Service History — including which generation (W638, W639 or W447) and evidence of major maintenance
3. Coolant Level — Any unexplained loss? (head gasket or thermostat housing warning)
4. Oil Leaks (M112) — Any oil under the car or around the engine? (PCV system or breather leaks)
5. Vibrations (M112) — Any unusual vibrations? (crank pulley disintegration warning)
6. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
7. Running Quality — Any rough idle or loss of power? (MAF, injector or PCV issues)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)
